title: Momentum and Newton's laws of motion
paperName: Paper 1: Multiple Choice / Paper 2: AS Level Structured Questions
summary: Master momentum, impulse and Newton's three laws for Cambridge 9702 AS Physics. Clear explanations, worked examples and exam tips included.
learningObjectives: Define linear momentum and impulse, and apply the impulse–momentum theorem to collisions and explosions
State and apply Newton's three laws of motion, including the concept of Newton pairs in the third law
Apply the principle of conservation of momentum to solve problems involving collisions and explosions in one and two dimensions

simpleExplanation.title: Momentum in Plain English
simpleExplanation.summary: Momentum is simply how hard it is to stop a moving object — it depends on both mass and velocity. Newton's laws tell us what causes momentum to change, and the principle of conservation of momentum tells us that in a closed system the total momentum never changes. Master these ideas and a huge chunk of Paper 1 and Paper 2 becomes very manageable.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of a shopping trolley: an empty one is easy to stop, but a fully loaded one at the same speed takes much more effort — that extra effort reflects the greater momentum. When two trolleys collide in a supermarket car park, the total momentum of the pair before the crash equals the total momentum after, no matter how messy the collision looks.
steps: Learn the definition: momentum p = mv (kg m s⁻¹) and make sure you always treat it as a vector
Connect force and momentum: F = Δp/Δt, so a net force changes momentum over time — this is Newton's second law in its most general form
Practise conservation of momentum: write total momentum before = total momentum after, being careful with signs for direction
Distinguish elastic (KE conserved) from inelastic (KE not conserved) collisions — Cambridge examiners test this distinction regularly

sections.content: Momentum is one of the most important quantities in all of physics, and Cambridge 9702 examiners love testing it. In this topic you will pin down exactly what **linear momentum** is, see how **Newton's three laws of motion** arise naturally from it, and learn to apply the powerful **principle of conservation of momentum** — a law that holds even when forces are impossibly complicated to measure directly. Get this topic right and you unlock marks across collision questions, force–time graphs, rocket problems and much more.

The **linear momentum** of an object is defined as the product of its mass and its velocity. Because velocity is a vector, momentum is also a vector — direction matters every single time you use it. The SI unit is kg m s⁻¹, which is equivalent to N s. In Cambridge mark schemes, either unit is accepted but kg m s⁻¹ is safest to write unless the question specifically involves impulse.

**Newton's First Law** states that an object remains at rest or continues to move with constant velocity unless acted upon by a resultant (net) external force. This is really a statement about momentum: if the net force is zero, momentum does not change.

**Newton's Second Law** in its most general and examinable form states that the net force acting on an object is equal to the rate of change of its momentum. For a constant mass this reduces to F = ma, but the momentum form is more powerful — it applies even when mass changes (e.g. a rocket burning fuel).

**Newton's Third Law** states that when object A exerts a force on object B, object B exerts a force on object A that is equal in magnitude, opposite in direction, and of the same type. These two forces — called a **Newton pair** — act on different objects, so they can never cancel each other out.

items: Momentum is a **vector**: always define a positive direction at the start of a problem and stick to it throughout — a sign error will cost you marks even if your method is correct
The general form of Newton's second law is F = Δp/Δt; only when mass is constant does this become F = ma — state this distinction if asked to 'explain' the second law
**Impulse** is defined as the product of force and the time for which it acts: J = FΔt = Δp. On a force–time graph, the impulse equals the area under the curve
For a **Newton's third law pair**, both forces must be the same type (e.g. both gravitational, both normal contact), equal in magnitude, opposite in direction, and acting on different objects
The **principle of conservation of momentum** applies to a **closed system** (no external net force): total momentum before = total momentum after. This holds for all collisions and explosions

In an **elastic collision** kinetic energy is conserved; in an **inelastic collision** kinetic energy is not conserved (some is transferred to heat, sound etc.). A **perfectly inelastic** collision is one where the objects stick together

sections.content: Impulse (symbol J) is defined as the product of the net force and the time interval over which it acts. Because F = Δp/Δt, it follows that J = FΔt = Δp. This means impulse equals the change in momentum — a result Cambridge examiners frequently ask you to derive or apply. When the force is not constant, the impulse is the **area under a force–time graph**. A common Paper 2 question shows a curved force–time graph and asks you to estimate the impulse by counting squares or using the trapezium rule.
Impulse J = FΔt = Δp  (unit: N s or kg m s⁻¹)

The principle of conservation of linear momentum states that the total linear momentum of a system remains constant provided no resultant external force acts on the system. This follows directly from Newton's third law: during a collision the forces the two objects exert on each other are equal and opposite, so the changes in their momenta are equal and opposite, leaving the total unchanged. This principle applies to explosions too — for example, when a gun fires a bullet, the gun and bullet both start at rest (total momentum = 0), so the bullet moves forward with the same magnitude of momentum as the gun recoils backward.

sections.question:
A railway wagon of mass 4000 kg moving at 3.0 m s⁻¹ due east collides with a stationary wagon of mass 6000 kg. After the collision the two wagons move together. 
(a) Calculate the velocity of the wagons immediately after the collision. 
(b) Show that kinetic energy is not conserved in this collision. 
(c) A student claims that momentum is not conserved because kinetic energy is lost. Explain why the student is wrong.
sections.solution:
**Define positive direction:** east = positive.

**Part (a) — Conservation of momentum:**
Total momentum before = (4000 × 3.0) + (6000 × 0) = 12 000 kg m s⁻¹
After collision the wagons stick together (perfectly inelastic), combined mass = 10 000 kg.
p_before = p_after
12 000 = 10 000 × v
v = 12 000 / 10 000 = **1.2 m s⁻¹ due east** ✓

**Part (b) — Check kinetic energy:**
KE before = ½ × 4000 × 3.0² = ½ × 4000 × 9 = **18 000 J**
KE after = ½ × 10 000 × 1.2² = ½ × 10 000 × 1.44 = **7 200 J**
Since 7 200 J ≠ 18 000 J, kinetic energy is not conserved. (10 800 J is converted to heat and sound.) ✓

**Part (c) — Why the student is wrong:**
Momentum and kinetic energy are different quantities. Momentum is conserved whenever there is no net external force on the system (Newton's third law guarantees this for the collision forces). Kinetic energy is a scalar and can be transformed into other forms of energy — this does not violate conservation of momentum, because momentum conservation and energy conservation are independent principles.

sections.content: **The most common mistake in collision questions is a sign error.** Always write down your chosen positive direction before you start, then assign signs consistently. If an object moves left and you defined right as positive, its momentum is negative. Cambridge mark schemes award a method mark for a correct conservation equation, but the answer mark requires the correct sign/direction — do not throw it away carelessly. Also, when a question asks you to 'state' Newton's third law, you must mention: (i) equal magnitude, (ii) opposite direction, (iii) same type of force, and (iv) acting on different objects — missing any one of these is a partial answer at best.
5. Collisions in Two Dimensions (Extension for P2)

For two-dimensional collisions, momentum is conserved **independently** in each direction. Resolve all momenta into two perpendicular components (typically x and y, or along and perpendicular to the initial direction of motion). Write a conservation equation for each component separately, then solve. This type of question sometimes appears in the later parts of a Paper 2 structured question and is worth practising with vector diagrams.

faq.q: What is the difference between momentum and impulse in Cambridge 9702 Physics?
faq.a: Momentum (p = mv) is the property of a moving object at any instant, measured in kg m s⁻¹. Impulse (J = FΔt) is the product of the net force and the time for which it acts — it equals the *change* in momentum, not the momentum itself. Cambridge examiners frequently ask students to define both and to calculate impulse from the area under a force–time graph. Our free revision course has worked examples and past-paper questions to help you master both concepts.

faq.q: How do I apply conservation of momentum to explosion questions in 9702?
faq.a: In an explosion, the system starts at rest so total initial momentum is zero. After the explosion the momenta of all fragments must still sum to zero, meaning the fragments move in opposite directions with equal magnitudes of momentum. For example, if a stationary object of mass M splits into two parts m₁ and m₂, then m₁v₁ = m₂v₂ in magnitude but opposite in direction. Practice this with our free past-paper marking tool and mark scheme walkthroughs at the link above.

faq.q: Why does Cambridge 9702 use F = Δp/Δt instead of F = ma for Newton's second law?
faq.a: F = ma only applies when mass is constant. The more general statement, F = Δp/Δt, applies even when mass is changing — for example, a rocket expelling exhaust gases or a conveyor belt gaining mass. Cambridge syllabuses at AS level require you to be able to use and explain both forms, and Paper 2 questions sometimes set up variable-mass scenarios where F = ma would be incorrect. Our premium lesson above explains this distinction clearly, and you can test yourself using our free past-paper resources.
